resume:
Staff Operations Engineer · 9 years scaling mission-critical infrastructure

- Strategically operationalized end-to-end production excellence across multi-region hyperscale workloads
- Architected best-in-class incident response paradigms, championing a culture of relentless reliability
- Led cross-functional war rooms to drive holistic root-cause synthesis at enterprise scale
- Empowered SRE squads to ship value-add automation, leveraging modern observability stacks
- Pioneered next-generation chaos engineering frameworks across the platform org
- Owned strategic vendor relationships with cloud and observability partners
- Drove a culture of psychological safety in postmortems through transparent learning loops

Most recent: Lattice Cloud (2022-present) — Staff Operations Engineer, Platform Reliability
Prior: Crystal Data (2018-2022) — Senior SRE
jd: 我们正在为 Lattice Cloud 平台可靠性团队招聘 Staff Operations Engineer。Reports to Director of Platform。You will own the on-call rotation for a 4-region Kubernetes fleet (~3000 nodes), drive the SLO/SLI program for tier-1 services (currently 5 services on a 99.95 budget), and lead at least one major reliability initiative per quarter. We need someone who has personally written runbooks that fired during real incidents, who has rolled back a bad deploy at 3am, and who can quantify the dollar impact of unplanned downtime. The team is recovering from a Q4 incident that cost ~$2.3M in SLO credits — your first 90 days will include a deep audit of our error-budget burn drivers. Travel <10%.

history.q: Walk me through your current scope at Lattice — what services do you own end-to-end?
Your resume mentions 'best-in-class incident response.' Pick one Sev1 you led last quarter and tell me about it.
What was the customer-visible impact of that incident — duration, requests affected, dollar SLO credits issued?
Who actually wrote the rollback script — you, your teammate, or a SRE on another team?
Your prior answer used 'we' a lot. Walk me through your individual contributions during that incident, hour by hour.
I want to make sure I understand — when you say you 'led' the response, were you the IC, the comms lead, or the on-call?
Last question on this — what is one decision you personally made during that Sev1 that, in hindsight, you wish you'd made differently?

candidate_last_answer: 我觉得这个问题有点 unfair。说实话, every Sev1 is a team effort — 你不能让 one engineer 承担 hindsight blame。如果你想要 individual heroics, that's actually an anti-pattern in modern SRE culture. 我做的 decisions 都是 based on best available info at the time, and the postmortem 也是这么 conclude 的。If you want me to nitpick something, fine — maybe 我应该 page 一下 the database team 更早 5 分钟? 但是 honestly that's nitpicking — overall response 是 textbook execution.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: maybe 我应该 page 一下 the database team 更早 5 分钟

expected-yield: a concrete named decision the candidate owned alone, with the alternative considered and the timing window — converts defensive 'it was textbook' into a specific 5-minute window of personal judgment
